coredata.dc:title: WO3·0.33H2O/carbon quantum dots hybrid nanostructures for efficient electrochemical hydrogen evolution reaction
coredata.dc:creator: Abraham, Angelin
Muhammed Anees, P.
Eldho, Aleena
Bushiri, M. Junaid
coredata.dc:description: Low-cost electrocatalysts with good catalytic performance are highly desirable for the sustainable production of hydrogen in order to meet the growing global energy crisis. In this work, WO3·0.33H2O/casein-derived carbon quantum dots hybrid nanostructures were synthesized via hydrothermal synthesis route utilizing casein-derived carbon quantum dots and precursor salts of WO3, and its electrochemical performance as an HER electrocatalyst was investigated. XRD, FESEM, TEM, SAED, XPS, FTIR, and Raman analyses confirmed the structural and compositional features of the WO3·0.33H2O/carbon quantum dots hybrid nanostructures. The electrochemical studies showed an efficient HER activity for WO3·0.33H2O/carbon quantum dots hybrid nanostructure with a low overpotential of 197 mV and a small Tafel slope of 101 mV/dec. The synergic effect of the hydrated tungsten oxide and carbon dots enhanced the conductivity and number of accessible active sites, promoting better mass transport, which was responsible for better HER activity. This work presents a cost-effective and green strategy to develop WO3·0.33H2O/carbon quantum dots hybrid nanostructures as a suitable electrocatalyst for hydrogen production.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrothermal synthesis
WO3/casein derived carbon quantum dots
Hydrogen evolution reaction

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge FIST, DST, Government of India and DST PURSE for the FESEM and EDX analysis at the Department of Physics, CUSAT. The authors thank DST SAIF- STIC Cochin, India, for FT-IR and HR-TEM analysis and CLIF, University of Kerala, India, for the XPS analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD pattern of the hydrothermally synthesized WO3·0.33H2O (W0) and WO3·0.33H2O/CQDs hybrid nanostructures (W1, W2, W3 and W4).
(a) TEM image (inset showing the particle size distribution curve), (b) HRTEM image, (c) SAED pattern of the W3 sample.

(a) FTIR spectra, (b) Raman spectra, (c) Raman spectra in the range 1200–1700 cm−1 of the WO3·0.33H2O/ CQDs composites obtained with varying amounts (0,1,2,3 and 4 mL) of CQDs solution along with the precursor salts of tungsten oxide by hydrothermal process.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of the W3 sample. High-resolution deconvolution peaks of (b)W 4f, (c) O1s and (d) C1s.

(a) LSV polarization curves of WO3·0.33H2O composites modified with different amounts of CQDs, (b) Tafel slopes derived from the LSV curves, (c) Plot depicting the double layer capacitance measurement (d) LSV curves normalized by the ECSA (e) Impedance spectra of the samples recorded at 0.5 M H2SO4 electrolyte (f) Chrono potentiometric stability measurements of the W3 sample at a constant current density of 10 mA/cm2 held for 24 h.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The HER parameters of the W3 sample with better HER activity are shown in bold.

head.ce:title: WO3·0.33H2O/carbon quantum dots hybrid nanostructures for efficient electrochemical hydrogen evolution reaction

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Cochin University of Science and Technology, Cochin, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Cochin University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Cochin University of Science and Technology, Cochin, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Low-cost electrocatalysts with good catalytic performance are highly desirable for the sustainable production of hydrogen in order to meet the growing global energy crisis. In this work, WO3·0.33H2O/casein-derived carbon quantum dots hybrid nanostructures were synthesized via hydrothermal synthesis route utilizing casein-derived carbon quantum dots and precursor salts of WO3, and its electrochemical performance as an HER electrocatalyst was investigated. XRD, FESEM, TEM, SAED, XPS, FTIR, and Raman analyses confirmed the structural and compositional features of the WO3·0.33H2O/carbon quantum dots hybrid nanostructures. The electrochemical studies showed an efficient HER activity for WO3·0.33H2O/carbon quantum dots hybrid nanostructure with a low overpotential of 197 mV and a small Tafel slope of 101 mV/dec. The synergic effect of the hydrated tungsten oxide and carbon dots enhanced the conductivity and number of accessible active sites, promoting better mass transport, which was responsible for better HER activity. This work presents a cost-effective and green strategy to develop WO3·0.33H2O/carbon quantum dots hybrid nanostructures as a suitable electrocatalyst for hydrogen production.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen is acknowledged as a clean and sustainable fuel having a high calorific value and gravimetric energy density of 142 MJ kg−1 and zero carbon emissions [1–4]. Electrochemical water splitting is considered a sustainable and renewable pathway to harness hydrogen and is a promising and clean alternative to the rely on non-renewable sources of energy. Fabrication of a suitable electrocatalyst for the large-scale production of hydrogen, therefore, has been gaining much research interest in order to compact the global energy crisis. Electrochemical water splitting involves two half-cell reactions with hydrogen evolution reactions (HER) at the cathode and oxygen evolution reactions (OER) at the anode [5–7]. Platinum group elements, such as Pt, Pd, Ru and Ir etc., are the most popular state-of-the-art catalyst for the electrochemical hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) with an overpotential value close to zero [8]. The commercialization of aforesaid HER catalysts is compromised because of their high cost, low abundance and durability [9]. However, from a practical application point of view, an ideal HER electrocatalyst should possess good electronic conductivity, low overpotential, small Tafel slopes, and high exchange current density [2]. Apart from that, a good electrocatalyst should be stabilized against leaching under harsh mediums of operation.

Interestingly, several Pt-free catalysts, such as metal oxides, carbides, phosphides, nitrides, sulphides, and transition metals and their alloys show better HER activity [8–13]. However, some of these materials are likely to corrode in an acidic medium which leads to electrochemical leaching of the electrocatalyst [14]. Among the transition metal oxides, tungsten trioxide (WO3) and its hydrates (WO3.nH2O) has been identified as efficient and cost-effective HER catalyst as they possess superior thermodynamic stability in both acidic and alkaline mediums with high redox capability. Tungsten oxide is an n-type semiconductor with a bandgap of ~2.8 eV, which has been primarily investigated for electrochromic smart windows, gas sensors, dye-sensitized solar cells, field emission and photocatalysis owing to its unique electrochromic, photochromic, catalytic, and electric properties [15]. In addition to this, WO3 can form tungsten bronze (HxWO4) with either coordinated or interlayer water molecules, which can promote proton conductivity and hence be favourable as an HER catalyst [14,16].

So far, several works have focussed on the HER activity of WO3 and its hydrates. The sluggish kinetics and poor electronic conductivity of WO3 can be further enhanced by tuning the morphologies of the material [17], modulating the atomic structure for oxygen vacancies [18,19], by compositing with metal composites. In addition to the above methods, incorporating carbonaceous materials such as graphene/graphene oxide and carbon nanotubes into the electrocatalyst is found to improve the surface area, active edge sites and electron transfer [20]. Moreover, in some cases, the active carbonaceous matrix can reduce the volumetric changes in WO3 during the electrochemical cycling and, in turn, improve the conductivity between the active material and the electrode [21]. Several works have reported the enhancement of HER activity of WO3 by hybridizing it with conductive agents such as CNT, carbon nanofibers and reduced graphene oxide [22–25,32]. Even though the carbonaceous materials endow metal oxides with promising electrochemical properties for energy-related applications, traditional protocols followed for synthesizing carbon materials are often tedious, requiring strong acids and a longer duration for the synthesis. In this respect, hydrothermal synthesis has recently attracted attention as a greener, sustainable, and cost-effective method for synthesizing carbonaceous materials from bio-sources without using any surfactants and organic solvents [26]. Notably, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are an attractive source of carbon support owing to their high electronic conductivity, redox properties, good stability, and abundance of sp3 and sp2 carbon bonds coupled with their non-toxic nature [27,28]. Generally, natural carbon precursors with amino and carboxyl groups are considered ideal for synthesizing self-doped carbon quantum dots with functionalized bioconjugates [29]. Amino acid-rich proteins are hence, an ideal choice as a precursor for the synthesis of carbon quantum dots. Among the various natural carbon sources, casein is a readily available dairy protein with high amount of amino acids [30]. The use of nitrogenous protein casein as the precursor for carbon quantum dots is likely to replace the use of expensive precursors. Indeed, hydrothermally synthesized carbon materials as an additive to hydrated WO3 can be a promising low-cost and effective electrocatalyst for energy applications. The present work demonstrates an effective strategy to develop a low-cost and eco-friendly electrocatalyst comprising hydrated tungsten oxide coupled with casein-derived CQDs in pursuit of economic hydrogen production for the first time.

Carbon quantum dots were synthesized by hydrothermal method using casein as a carbon source and adapted from the literature [30]. 1 g of casein was dissolved in 30 mL of KOH solution such that the ratio of casein to KOH is 1:1. The solution was poured into a Teflon beaker, kept inside a stainless-steel autoclave, and heated at 160 °C for 24 h. After hydrothermal treatment, the resulting casein-derived carbon quantum dots (CQDs) solution was centrifuged and stored until further use.

In a typical synthesis procedure, 2.5 g of sodium tungstate dihydrate was dissolved in 40 mL Millipore water, followed by the addition of concentrated HCl for acidic pH conditions. The final solution was magnetically stirred and then transferred to a 100 mL Teflon beaker and afterwards autoclaved at 160 °C in a stainless-steel autoclave. After 24 h of heating, the autoclave was left to cool down naturally, and the precipitates were collected, washed with water and ethanol, and dried at 60 °C (designated as W0). For the synthesis of the WO3·0.33H2O/CQDs samples, different quantities of previously prepared casein-derived carbon quantum dots solution (1,2,3,4 mL) were added to the aforesaid precursor solution of the WO3·0.33H2O and the synthesis was performed as described. The WO3·0.33H2O sample synthesized with different amounts of CQDs, viz., 1,2,3 and 4 mL, were labelled as W1, W2, W3 and W4, respectively.

The X-ray powder diffraction of the samples was obtained by using a PANalytical XPERT-PRO X-ray diffractometer with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.5405 Å). FESEM images to study the morphology of the samples were analyzed using Carl Zeiss sigma FESEM. TEM Jeol/JEM 2100 was used for the structural characterizations with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. FTIR data of the samples were obtained with the KBr pellet technique and recorded using a Nicolet iS50 FT-IR spectrometer with a DTGS-KBr detector in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. Raman spectra were measured using Horiba JOBIN YVON LabRAM HR Raman Spectrometer with an excitation source of a wavelength of 514.5 nm. Thermo ScientificTM ESCALABTM Xi+ with Al-Kα monochromatic X-Ray radiation (1486.6 eV) as the X-Ray source was used for the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) analysis.

Electrochemical analysis was carried out on a three-electrode CHI-6054E electrochemical workstation with glassy carbon, Ag|AgCl and Pt wire as working (WE), reference (RE) and counter electrode (CE), respectively. To prepare the electrocatalytic ink, 10 mg of the prepared sample was added to 1 mL ethanol and 10 μL of Nafion and ultrasonically dispersed for 30 min. Before loading the catalyst, the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was polished with alumina powder. Further, five microlitres of the electrocatalytic ink were drop-casted on a GCE with 3 mm diameter. Finally, the coated working electrode was dried at room temperature and then tested using 0.5 M H2SO4 electrolyte. The electrode potential measured against the Ag|AgCl electrode was calibrated against RHE using the following equation [56],
(1)
E
RHE
= E
Ag AgCl
+ 0.19 + 0.0591 × pH

Fig. 1 shows the XRD pattern of the samples under investigation, and all the samples are crystalline with characteristic 2θ peaks at 14.1°, 18.1°, 23.1°, 24.2°, 27.1° and 28.1° which corresponds to the (020), (111), (002), (200), (131) and (220) crystal planes of orthorhombic WO3·0.33H2O (JCPDS 01-072-0199). No crystalline carbon-related peaks are observed in the XRD pattern of the W1, W2, W3 and W4, in which carbon quantum dots solution is added to the precursor solution for the hydrothermal synthesis. The absence of carbon-related peaks in the XRD pattern may be ascribed to the relatively small carbon content, low diffraction intensity and limited restacking behaviour of the CQDs [26,32].

Also, as the amount of carbon quantum dots solution added to the precursor growth medium is increased, an enhancement in the relative intensity of the (220) plane of WO3·0.33H2O is observed in the XRD patterns. This preferential growth of WO3·0.33H2O along the (220) plane can be attributed to the interaction of the precursor of the WO3 with the functional groups present in the carbon quantum dots [26,33]. FESEM images of the hydrated WO3 with 3 mL of CQDs (W3) are depicted in Fig. S1(f) (Supporting Information). The elemental mapping, shown in Fig. S1(e), revealed a uniform distribution of the C element on the WO3·0.33H2O nanostructures. In addition, the EDX analysis revealed the presence of W, O and C in the samples.

Fig. 2a shows the TEM image of the W3 sample, revealing nanoplates with a lateral width of 7–8 nm and a length of a few nanometres. The interplanar spacing of 0.31 nm corresponds to the (220) lattice fringe of the WO3·0.33H2O, which is in accordance with the XRD results. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (Fig. 2c) shows the polycrystalline ring structure indicating the nanocrystalline nature of the W3 sample.

Fig. 3a. represents the FTIR spectra of the W0, W1, W2, W3 and W4 samples. A broad IR band is seen below 1000 cm−1, which is related to the vibrations of the O-W-O bond [34]. A shoulder band at 1006 cm−1 corresponds to the WO valence vibration. A broad IR band at 682 cm−1 corresponds to the v(O-W-O) vibration [35]. No carbon-related peaks are seen in the IR spectra of the samples except for the W3 and W4 samples. The weak IR band at 1463 cm−1 in the W3 and W4 sample can be ascribed to the stretching vibrations of CH2 groups. In addition, the weak band observed at 1404 cm−1 of the W3 and W4 samples represents the CN stretching vibration, which signifies the presence of functionalized carbon quantum dots [36,37]. The broad band around 3460 cm−1 indicates the OH stretching vibration of the H2O, while the band at 1606 cm−1 can be ascribed to the bending mode of the water molecules [34,35]. The interlayer water in the W3 sample plays an important role in enhancing the HER activity in the WO3·0.33H2O electrocatalyst.

Furthermore, in Fig. 3b, intense Raman bands at 681 and 806 cm−1 represent the O-W-O stretching mode which is almost similar to those previously reported [21]. The Raman band at 949 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching mode of WO and is the characteristic peak of hydrated WO3 [38,39], which is in agreement with our XRD data. Fig. 3c shows the presence of weak D band and G band at 1377 and 1569 cm−1 in the spectra of samples W3 and W4, which correspond to the A1g vibrational mode of the sp3 carbon of the disordered graphite and E2 g mode of the sp2 carbon [39,40,41]. The Raman and FTIR observations confirm the hydrothermal carbonization of casein and its integration into the WO3·0.33H2O samples.

In addition, the degree of disorder was estimated from the ID/IG ratio, obtained for W3 and W4 as 1.04 and 0.89, respectively. The ID/IG ratio of 1.04 indicates a higher disorder and enhanced defects in W3 than in W4.

Fig. 4a depicts the XPS survey profile and confirms the elemental composition with signals of W 4f, O 1s and C 1s at 36.4, 530.7 and 284 eV, respectively. The XPS spectrum of W, 4f state has two doublets indicating W6+ and W5+ states of the tungsten. The deconvoluted XPS peaks located at 37.6 and 35.5 eV can be ascribed to the 4f5/2 and 4f7/2, respectively, indicating the presence of the W6+ valence state of the WO bond. The two deconvoluted peaks at 36.6 and 34.7 eV correspond to the 4f5/2 and 4f7/2 of the W5+ state from the WO bond. The existence of the WO bond is confirmed from the IR spectrum, as evidenced by the IR band at 1006 cm−1 and the Raman band at 949 cm−1. Besides, the coexistence of two spin-orbit doublet states of W, viz. W6+ and W5+ indicate the presence of oxygen vacancies [14,31]. In this case, the W5+ oxidation state is likely to enhance the electron density in the W cation. The presence of an unpaired electron in the d0 orbital can be transferred to the adsorbate, which will act as active sites for the adsorption of protons and hence improve the electrocatalytic activity. The O 1s has deconvoluted peaks 530.7 and 532.1 eV, which is attributed to the OW and physisorbed water W-O-H bonds, respectively, which is in agreement with the FTIR results. Moreover, the third peak at 534.9 eV may be ascribed to the OC bond. The C 1s high-resolution spectrum has peaks at 288.7, 286.4 and 284.7 eV after deconvolution, which is associated with the sp2 N=C-N, CO and the sp2 CC functional groups, confirming the integration of CQDs in the WO3·0.33H2O in conformity with previously described Raman and EDX results.

In order to evaluate the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), all samples were tested in 0.5 M H2SO4 electrolyte solution. The linear sweep voltammograms were measured in the potential range of 0 to −0.6 V (RHE) at a slow scan rate of 5 mV/s with a view to reducing the non-Faradaic current (Fig. 5a). The polarization curve of the Pt electrode, for comparison, displayed an excellent overpotential of 32 mV to attain a current density of 10 mA/cm2. Notably, the HER activity is increased in the order of samples W4 < W0 < W1 < W2 < W3. The highest HER activity is exhibited by the W3 sample, with a lower overpotential of −197 mV compared to WO3·0.33H2O, which required an overpotential of −427 mV to obtain a current density of 10 mA/cm2. The improved electrocatalytic HER is attributed to the presence of carbon quantum dots that facilitate the electron transfer between the cathode and the electrolyte [42]. The experimental results show a considerable decrease in the evolution of hydrogen bubbles for sample W4 indicating the reduction in HER activity on a further increase of the carbon dot quantity in the WO3·0.33H2O sample.

The reduction in hydrogen evolution in W4, having relatively more CQDs content, can be ascribed to the piling up of the carbon quantum dots on the surface of the WO3·0.33H2O electrocatalyst, which in turn reduces the availability of active sites and hence cause probable hindrance in mass transfer [43]. The kinetic performance of the catalyst was evaluated with the Tafel slope obtained by fitting the LSV data with the Tafel equation,
(2)
η = a + b log ∣ j ∣
where η is the overpotential, “a” is a constant, “b” is the Tafel slope, and “j” is the current density [40]. The Tafel slopes are obtained as 101 mV/dec for the W3 sample (Fig. 5b), which is much lower than the Tafel values of the other samples, demonstrating faster kinetics. In addition, the Tafel slope is useful in the elucidation of the rate-determining step for HER. Typically, the hydrogen evolution reaction proceeds via two steps, namely, Volmer and Heyrovsky or Tafel step [40]. In an acidic medium, the initial step is the Volmer step, where a proton from the electrolyte is captured by an electron from the circuit to form adsorbed hydrogen (Hads) intermediate. The next step involves the evolution of H2, which can be either through the Heyrovsky or Tafel step [40,42,44]. The former takes place when the Hads coverage is low and proceeds by electron or proton transfer with Hads. The latter is the evolution of H2 when the Hads coverage is high by the recombination of two adjacent Hads. Compared to acidic HER, alkaline HER has sluggish kinetics as it proceeds differently with water as the donor due to the lack of H+. The three steps in electrocatalytic HER in the acidic electrolyte are given by Eqs. (3), (4), and (5) [44],
(3)
H 3
O +
+
e −
→
H ads
+
H 2
O
(4)
H ads
+
H 3
O +
+
e −
→
H 2
+
H 2
O
(5)
H ads
+
H ads
→
H 2

The Tafel slope of 101 mV/dec indicates a Volmer-Heyrovsky pathway as the rate-limiting step [46]. The electrochemical HER process of the W3 sample involves the adsorption of the H+ species on the electrode surface via a discharge step (Volmer step) followed by electrochemical desorption of the intermediate adsorbed hydrogen (Hads) to produce H2 into the solution (Heyrovsky step) [45]. The synergic effect of WO3·0.33H2O and the carbon quantum dots in enhancing the HER activity may be due to the ability of tungsten oxide to form tungsten bronzes (WO3·nH2O + x(H++e−) → HxWO3·nH2O) [18,47–49] and the presence of weakly bonded water molecules, which favour the HER activity by faster proton diffusion [16]. Moreover, the generated intermediate Hads is likely to combine with a hydrated proton from WO3·0.33H2O and electrons provided by the CQDs on the electrode surface to generate H2. The exchange current density (j0) is the current density at zero overpotential, obtained from the Tafel equation with overpotential η = 0 V vs RHE (Fig. S2) and is another vital descriptor of the reaction rates and charge transfer at the electrode surface [40]. The exchange current density of the W3 sample is yielded as 0.015 A/cm2, which is higher than that obtained for the W0 (0.36 × 10−3 A/cm2), W1 (0.69 × 10−3 A/cm2), W2 (1.3 × 10−3 A/cm2) and W4 (0.2 × 10−3 A/cm2). The results coincide with the fact that W3 is a better HER electrocatalyst than the other samples under investigation.

In order to estimate the electrochemical surface area (ECSA), the double-layer capacitance (Cdl) is measured from the CV curves at different scan rates in the non-Faradaic region with no redox peaks (Fig. S3). The calculation of ECSA and Cdl values will provide insight into the intrinsic activity and number of electrochemically active sites [50–52]. The plot of the difference in anodic and cathodic current densities against different scan rates yields a straight line whose slope gives the Cdl value (Fig. 5c). It is observed that the Cdl value for the W3 sample is 0.148 mF/cm2 with an ESCA of 3.72 and a roughness factor (Rf) of 53. All the values are higher than W0, W1, W2, and W4 samples (Table 1 ), suggesting a higher number of accessible active sites and enhanced electrocatalytic activity. All the calculated HER parameters are summarised in Table 1. Fig. 5d represents the LSV curves normalized by electrochemical surface area (ECSA) instead of the geometric surface area. The ECSA-normalized LSV curves give insight into the intrinsic activity of the synthesized electrocatalysts [53,54]. Although a difference in overpotential is observed in the normalized and non-normalized HER polarization curves, the HER activity of the catalysts maintained similar trends as that observed in geometric surface area- normalized LSV curves. The W3 sample exhibited better intrinsic activity in the LSV after ECSA normalization. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed at frequencies 0.1 Hz to 106 Hz to further elucidate the charge transfer kinetics between the electrode-electrolyte interface in HER. Fig. 5e represents the Nyquist plot of all the samples, and among all the samples, W3 shows the smallest resistive-semicircle diameter indicating a smaller charge-transfer resistance [55,56]. The charge transfer resistance (Rct) of the W3 sample is estimated as 28 Ω. This demonstrates a faster charge transfer during the redox reaction on the electrode surface.

Furthermore, to evaluate the stability of the W3 HER catalyst, chronopotentiometry measurements are done in 0.5 M H2SO4 electrolyte at a cathodic current density of 10 mA/cm2 for 24 h, as shown in Fig. 5f. Although a slight drift is observed initially which can be attributed to the enhanced activation of the catalytic sites, the W3 sample still exhibited fairly good stability with vigorous evolution of hydrogen bubbles. The chronopotentiometry durability results suggest that the W3 sample can be used as an electrocatalyst for HER. For post-HER microstructural analysis, the W3 from the GCE is dispersed in 1 mL ethanol via ultrasonication. From this dispersion, a drop is then drop-casted on the TEM grid. Fig. S4a depicts the HRTEM image of the W3 sample after the chronopotentiometry measurement and shows lattice fringes with a d-spacing of 0.31 nm, confirming the stability of the WO3·0.33H2O electrocatalyst. The crystallinity of the electrocatalyst is unaffected even after harsh electrochemical tests, as revealed by the SAED diffraction rings, from which the robustness of the WO3·0.33H2O/CQDs can be evidenced.

The low onset and overpotential, small Tafel slope, larger values of double layer capacitance, ESCA and roughness factor all are in good agreement with our observation and complement the efficiency of the W3 sample as a HER catalyst when compared to W0, W1, W2 and W4 samples.

In summary, we report the hydrothermal synthesis of WO3·0.33H2O/CQDs hybrid nanostructures utilizing casein-derived carbon quantum dots and precursor salts of WO3. XRD, FE-SEM, EDX, TEM, XPS, FTIR, and Raman analyses reveal the formation of WO3·0.33H2O/CQDs composites. Owing to a smaller Tafel slope of 101 mV/dec, lower overpotential of 197 mV, and high exchange current density of 0.015A/cm2 in the acidic medium, the WO3·0.33H2O/CQDs composites (W3) performed as a better electrocatalyst for the hydrogen evolution reaction compared to other contrast samples (W1, W2, W4). The better HER performance of the W3 sample can be attributed to the synergic effect of the hydrated WO3 and carbon quantum dots, which promotes faster H+ intercalation and electron transport between the electrode and electrolyte. The present investigation reveals a strategy to design WO3·0.33H2O/CQDs hybrid nanostructures as an efficient candidate for economical hydrogen production.

Angelin Abraham: Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Muhammed Anees P.: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Aleena Eldho: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. M. Junaid Bushiri: Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge FIST, DST, Government of India and DST PURSE for the FESEM and EDX analysis at the Department of Physics, CUSAT. The authors thank DST SAIF- STIC Cochin, India, for FT-IR and HR-TEM analysis and CLIF, University of Kerala, India, for the XPS analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2023.110309.

Int. J. Hydrog. Energy
